name: Milky Way
description: The band of our own galaxy arches across the sky on clear, dark nights. Summer months reveal the galactic core toward Sagittarius — the most star-rich region visible from Earth.

name: Ursa Major
description: Home of the famous Big Dipper asterism. Circumpolar from most of the United States — visible all year long. The two pointer stars guide you directly to Polaris.

name: Ursa Minor
description: Polaris, the North Star, sits at the tip of the Little Dipper's handle — the near-stationary pivot point around which all other stars appear to rotate.

name: Cassiopeia
description: The distinctive W or M shape is easily recognized near the north pole. It lies in a dense portion of the Milky Way, rich with star clusters.

name: Orion
description: One of the most iconic constellations — three belt stars, red supergiant Betelgeuse, and the famous Orion Nebula visible to the naked eye from dark sites.

name: Taurus
description: Contains two of the sky's finest star clusters — the Pleiades (Seven Sisters) and the Hyades — plus the Crab Nebula supernova remnant.

name: Gemini
description: Castor and Pollux are the twin 'heads' of Gemini. Castor is actually a sextuple star system. M35 is a fine open cluster at the constellation's foot.

name: Canis Major
description: Contains Sirius — the brightest star in the entire night sky at magnitude -1.46. It blazes blue-white just 8.6 light-years away.

name: Auriga
description: Capella is the 6th brightest star — actually two yellow giant stars orbiting each other. Three rich open clusters dot the constellation.

name: Leo
description: The backwards question-mark 'Sickle' traces the lion's mane. Regulus anchors it. Below lies the Leo Triplet — three interacting galaxies visible with binoculars.

name: Virgo
description: Follow the arc of the Big Dipper's handle to reach Arcturus, then 'spike' to Spica in Virgo. Behind Virgo lies a supercluster of 1,500+ galaxies.

name: Boötes
description: Arc from the Big Dipper's handle to find orange Arcturus — the brightest star in the northern sky and one of the nearest at 37 light-years.
features: Orange hue visible to naked eye

name: Corona Borealis
description: A perfect semicircle of stars like a jeweled crown. Small and easy to spot between Hercules and Boötes.

name: Hercules
description: Contains M13 — the Great Globular Cluster, one of the finest in the Northern Hemisphere, containing roughly 300,000 stars packed into 145 light-years.

name: Lyra
description: Vega — the 5th brightest star in the night sky — blazes at one corner of the famous Summer Triangle. The Ring Nebula shows a dying star's shell.

name: Cygnus
description: The Northern Cross flies along the Milky Way. Deneb — its tail star — is one of the most luminous stars known, shining like a million suns from 1,500 light-years away.

name: Aquila
description: Altair, the Summer Triangle's southernmost star, is only 17 light-years away and spins so fast it bulges visibly at its equator.

name: Sagittarius
description: Points toward the center of our galaxy — the Milky Way is densest here, packed with nebulae and clusters. The Teapot asterism is easy to recognize on the southern horizon.
features: Lagoon Nebula M8 (naked eye, dark sky)

name: Scorpius
description: A stunning summer constellation with vivid red Antares — a red supergiant so large it would engulf Mars' orbit — at its heart. Best from southern parks.

name: Perseus
description: Algol, the 'Demon Star', dims noticeably every 2.87 days as a companion eclipses it — visible to the naked eye! The Double Cluster is one of the sky's finest sights.

name: Andromeda
description: Home to M31 — the Andromeda Galaxy — the most distant object visible to the naked eye at 2.5 million light-years. On a truly dark night it appears larger than six full moons.
features: M31 Andromeda Galaxy (naked eye, dark sky)

name: Draco
description: A long circumpolar constellation coiling around Ursa Minor. Thuban was Earth's North Star 5,000 years ago when the pyramids were built.

name: Centaurus
description: Contains Alpha Centauri — our nearest stellar neighbor at 4.37 light-years — and Omega Centauri, the largest globular cluster visible from Earth. Viewable only from Florida, Hawaii, and southern Texas parks.
features: Only visible from southernmost US parks

name: Crux
description: Visible only from the southernmost US parks — Florida, Hawaii, and parts of Texas. The iconic Southern Cross points toward the south celestial pole and frames the Jewel Box cluster.
features: Visible from S. Florida & Hawaii only

name: Piscis Austrinus
description: Fomalhaut is the 'Loneliest Bright Star' — no other bright stars surround it in the autumn southern sky. It has a confirmed dusty exoplanet debris disk photographed by Hubble.
